summary: Small assembly-only fix for a graphics-breaking pointer issue in sysdolphin baselib psdisptev. The PR changed psdisptev.s to reference the raw address 0x80100480 instead of the symbol lbl_80100480, and removed the global export for lbl_80100480 from ftkirby.s, avoiding an invalid cross-file label/pointer dependency.

key_files.reason: Updated func_803A3A5C pointer construction from lbl_80100480@ha/@l to literal 0x80100480@ha/@l.
Removed the .global declaration for lbl_80100480 while leaving the local label itself in place.

what_changed: In asm/sysdolphin/baselib/psdisptev.s, func_803A3A5C no longer uses lbl_80100480 as the relocation target for lis/addi.
The psdisptev pointer setup now uses 0x80100480@ha and 0x80100480@l directly.
In asm/melee/ft/ftkirby.s, the blank line and `.global lbl_80100480` declaration before lbl_80100480 were removed.
The body states this resolves a shift that was breaking graphics.
smart_moves: Kept the machine-code address intent explicit in psdisptev by using the raw address when the symbolic reference was unsafe.
Removed the exported global label rather than keeping a misleading cross-module symbol that could be treated as a valid pointer target.
Made a minimal two-file assembly edit: only the pointer relocation reference and unnecessary global label export were touched.
decomp_lessons: Do not assume every label that happens to have an address is safe to export and reference across unrelated assembly files.
For assembly slices, a symbolic label can affect relocation/link behavior even if the intended numeric address is unchanged.
When fixing graphics regressions caused by shifted pointers, inspect whether labels introduced in one asm file are being consumed as external relocation targets elsewhere.

If the original code appears to require an absolute address rather than a real symbol dependency, using an explicit address may be the safer matching tactic until the surrounding code is better understood.
naming_conventions: Local labels in asm use the `lbl_XXXXXXXX` address-based form, as seen with `lbl_80100480`.
Functions in asm use the `func_XXXXXXXX` address-based form, as seen with `func_803A3A5C` and `func_8006EBA4`.
Small data symbols use address-based names such as `lbl_804D7950`.
assembly_or_matching_tactics: Replaced `addi r6, r4, lbl_80100480@l` with `addi r6, r4, 0x80100480@l`.
Removed `.global lbl_80100480` so the ftkirby label is no longer advertised as an external symbol.
Preserved the actual label `lbl_80100480:` in ftkirby.s, indicating the fix targeted symbol visibility/reference behavior rather than deleting code.
follow_up_queries: Find other assembly files that reference labels from unrelated modules with @ha/@l relocations.
Search for `.global lbl_` declarations that may have been added only to satisfy cross-file references.
Audit graphics-related asm pointers that use symbolic labels where a raw address may have been intended.
Look for later decompilation of func_803A3A5C or psdisptev to see how the 0x80100480 pointer was represented in C.
